    list(raw = "r.squared",     clean = "R²",      fmt = 3),
    list(raw = "adj.r.squared", clean = "Adj. R²", fmt = 3)
  ),
  title = "Table 12. Reduced-Form LPM: Pr(Owner-Occupied) — 17 Constituencies vs. 10 Districts"
) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "scale_down"), font_size = 9)
```

*Notes:* Outcome: 1 = household is owner-occupied (tenure codes 1–6 in 2022; 1–2 in 2010). Instrument: NTE × arrivals shift ($\Delta g = 0.187$) × Post. Geographic and year FEs absorbed. Census household weights. Clustered SEs. The negative coefficient indicates that owner-occupancy fell, rather than rose, in high-NTE constituencies relative to low-NTE constituencies during the STR era. \* p<0.10 \*\* p<0.05 \*\*\* p<0.01.

The negative coefficient on owner-occupancy in Table 12 ($\hat\beta$ = `r round(rq3_beta, 3)`, p `r fmt_p(rq3_p)`; district-level $\hat\beta$ = `r round(rq3_beta_dist, 3)`, p `r fmt_p(rq3_p_dist)`) runs counter to the simultaneous in-migrant-renter/investor-buyer sorting process hypothesised above. Instead, it suggests a single tenure-conversion channel: as renter rates rose in the highest-NTE constituencies between 2010 and 2022, owner-occupancy fell rather than rose, implying that previously owner-occupied or rent-free housing stock was converted into rental (and STR) units rather than new owner-occupier demand being drawn into these constituencies.

### RQ4: Crowding — Secondary Market Distortions

If displaced renters are pushed into lower-cost dwellings in peripheral constituencies, crowding might be expected to fall in high-NTE areas as the household composition shifts toward less-crowded owner-occupiers and in-migrant workers. Figure 9 and Table 13 test this prediction; the results below are inconclusive.

```{r fig-scatter-ppbr, fig.cap="Pre-STR Natural Tourism Endowment vs. Change in Persons per Bedroom, 2010–2022. Each point is one constituency. Source: CSO Saint Lucia Census 2010, 2022; NTE from OSM / Overpass API (May 2026).", fig.height=5}
cons_wide |>
  mutate(tourism_tier = factor(tourism_tier, levels = c("High", "Medium", "Low"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = nte, y = d_ppbr, label = constituency, color = tourism_tier)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE,
              color = "grey50", fill = "grey85", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text_repel(size = 2.5, max.overlaps = 17, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("High" = "#D7191C", "Medium" = "#FDAE61", "Low" = "#2C7BB6"),
    name = "Tourism Tier"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Pre-STR Natural Tourism Endowment vs. Change in Persons per Bedroom",
    subtitle = "Sign is sensitive to geographic aggregation; neither estimate is significant (Table 13)",
    x        = "Natural Tourism Endowment (NTE, pre-STR share)",
    y        = "Change in Persons per Bedroom 2010–2022",
    caption  = "Source: CSO Saint Lucia Census 2010, 2022; NTE from OSM / Overpass API (May 2026)."
  )
```

```{r table-rq4}
modelsummary(
  list("17 Constituencies" = lpm_ppbr, "10 Districts" = lpm_ppbr_dist),
  stars       = c("*" = 0.10, "**" = 0.05, "***" = 0.01),
  coef_rename = c(
    nte_post      = "NTE × Arrivals Shift × Post",
    nte_post_dist = "NTE × Arrivals Shift × Post"
  ),
  gof_map = list(
    list(raw = "nobs",          clean = "N",       fmt = 0),
    list(raw = "r.squared",     clean = "R²",      fmt = 3),
    list(raw = "adj.r.squared", clean = "Adj. R²", fmt = 3)
  ),
  title = "Table 13. Reduced-Form OLS: Persons per Bedroom — 17 Constituencies vs. 10 Districts"
) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "scale_down"), font_size = 9)
```

*Notes:* Outcome: persons per bedroom (continuous crowding measure). Instrument: NTE × arrivals shift ($\Delta g = 0.187$) × Post. Geographic and year FEs absorbed. Census household weights. Clustered SEs. The constituency-level estimate is positive and the district-level estimate is negative; neither is statistically significant, so this specification does not provide reliable evidence of a crowding effect in either direction. \* p<0.10 \*\* p<0.05 \*\*\* p<0.01.

## Discussion

The four sets of results point to a single dominant channel — rising renter rates in tourism-exposed constituencies — rather than the multi-channel gentrification narrative originally hypothesised in Section 3. Constituencies with the highest natural amenity endowments — Soufrière, Gros Islet, and Anse-la-Raye/Canaries — experienced the largest and most significant increases in renter populations between 2010 and 2022, alongside the highest median rents on the island. Owner-occupancy in these same constituencies fell rather than rose, and the relationship between tourism exposure and household crowding is not statistically robust. Together these patterns suggest that STR-era tourism intensification operated primarily by converting owner-occupied and informally-held housing stock into rental housing, rather than simultaneously attracting in-migrant renters and investor-buyers while displacing higher-crowding households outward, as the theoretical framework in Section 3 anticipated.

**The reduced form is the study's most credible causal result.** The Bartik instrument strongly predicts renting outcomes ($\hat{\beta}^{RF}$ = `r round(rf_beta, 3)`, cluster SE = `r round(rf_se, 3)`, Wald F = `r round(rf_waldf, 2)`, p `r fmt_p(rf_p)`) without relying on the first stage. The first-stage F-statistics are below the conventional threshold of 10, reflecting the small number of constituencies (17), and the 2SLS results should be read with caution. The reduced-form coefficient estimates the total causal effect of the Bartik-induced increase in tourism-corridor economic activity on renter rates, encompassing all channels through which NTE-correlated intensification affects tenure composition.

**The urban employment confound is the study's primary limitation.** Gros Islet and Castries are not only Saint Lucia's primary tourism corridors — they are also its principal urban employment centres. Workers relocating to these constituencies for employment in government agencies, hospitality operations, and commercial services would register as new renters in the 2022 census regardless of whether their jobs are directly linked to STR platform activity. Isolating the specific contribution of STR platforms from this broader economic intensification requires constituency-level labour force data that are not currently available. Future research combining census microdata with employment records from the National Insurance Corporation would allow this decomposition.

**Comparison with the international literature.** The directional pattern of findings is consistent with the broader STR literature: rents are higher and renter populations are larger in areas with greater platform penetration (Barron et al., 2021; Garcia-López et al., 2020). The magnitude of the estimated rent premium (approximately `r round(rent_premium_pct, 0)`%) is larger than most estimates from large cities, consistent with the expectation that small, thin markets with limited substitution opportunities will exhibit more pronounced price effects. The decline in owner-occupancy alongside rising renter rates points to a tenure-conversion mechanism — owner-occupied and informally-held housing shifting into the rental market — that is related to, but distinct from, the investor-driven "tourism gentrification" mechanism documented by Gotham (2005) in other tourism-driven urban contexts.

\newpage

# Conclusion

## Summary of Findings

This study has examined the relationship between short-term rental platform expansion and housing market outcomes across Saint Lucia's 17 electoral constituencies, using a Bartik shift-share instrumental variable design applied to full-population census microdata from 2010 and 2022. Four findings correspond to the four research questions.

**Finding 1 — Stock Depletion (RQ2):** Constituencies with greater pre-existing natural amenity endowments experienced significantly larger increases in renter populations during the STR platform era ($\hat{\beta}^{RF}$ = `r round(rf_beta, 3)`, p `r fmt_p(rf_p)`). This is the study's primary and most statistically robust result, and it holds across both geographic units (constituencies and districts) and both STR measurement strategies (narrow and broad DwellingStatus codes).

**Finding 2 — Rent Inflation (RQ1):** Renters in high-NTE constituencies face a rent premium of approximately `r round(rent_premium_pct, 0)`% relative to the lowest-NTE constituencies, conditional on dwelling size and household composition. This estimate is economically large but statistically underpowered at the 17-cluster level (p `r fmt_p(rent_nte_p)`); a longer rent panel is needed to confirm it.

**Finding 3 — Tenure-Shift Externality (RQ3):** Owner-occupancy rates *fell*, rather than rose, in the highest-NTE constituencies relative to low-NTE areas ($\hat\beta$ = `r round(rq3_beta, 3)`, p `r fmt_p(rq3_p)`; district-level $\hat\beta$ = `r round(rq3_beta_dist, 3)`, p `r fmt_p(rq3_p_dist)`), suggesting that STR-era tourism pressure converted owner-occupied and informally-held housing into rental stock rather than simultaneously attracting new owner-occupier and investor-buyer demand as originally hypothesised.

**Finding 4 — Crowding (RQ4):** The relationship between tourism exposure and household crowding is not statistically robust: the constituency-level estimate is positive ($\hat\beta$ = `r round(rq4_beta, 3)`, p `r fmt_p(rq4_p)`) while the district-level estimate is negative ($\hat\beta$ = `r round(rq4_beta_dist, 3)`, p `r fmt_p(rq4_p_dist)`), and neither reaches conventional significance. This analysis therefore does not provide reliable evidence of a crowding effect in either direction.

## Policy Recommendations

The findings have direct implications for housing policy in Saint Lucia. Eight targeted interventions are recommended, ordered by the directness of the policy-evidence link.

**1. Earmark Tourism Levy Revenue for Affordable Housing.** The government should earmark a share of Tourism Levy Act (2020) collections for affordable housing in the highest-NTE constituencies — Soufrière, Gros Islet, and Anse-la-Raye/Canaries — where this study documents that rents are highest and renter populations have grown fastest. This directly addresses the evidence of affordability pressures concentrated in tourism corridors.

**2. Resident-First Tax Incentives.** Tax relief under the Community Tourism Development Act (2022) — on income tax, property tax, and stamp duty — should be made conditional on the provider maintaining primary residence in Saint Lucia. This directly addresses the tenure-conversion mechanism identified in this research, whereby previously owner-occupied and informally-held housing in tourism corridors is converted into rental and STR stock, by ensuring that any resulting STR income accrues to resident, rather than absentee, property owners.

**3. Tax Exemption for Long-Term Rental Income.** Making rental income from genuine long-term residential leases tax-free would directly improve the financial attractiveness of the long-term rental market relative to STR platforms, counteracting the income differential that currently incentivises conversion. The `r round(rent_premium_pct, 0)`% descriptive rent premium in the highest-NTE constituencies demonstrates the scale of the market distortion such a reform would need to counteract.

**4. Tourism Decentralisation.** The Community Tourism Agency should steer visitors toward off-anchor constituencies — Micoud South, Choiseul, Laborie — to relieve housing cost pressures in Soufrière and Gros Islet while generating economic activity in underserved peripheral areas. This recommendation is directly motivated by the geographic concentration of the study's findings.

**5. Mandatory STR Quality Standards and Registration.** The Community Tourism Agency should use its authority to issue Operating Manuals mandating minimum standards for registered STR units, professionalising the sector while making compliance costs legible for enforcement. The study's census-based STR measure (245 buildings) is substantially smaller than the Airbtics-reported island-wide count (1,529 active listings in 2026), implying that many units may be operating without formal registration.

**6. Strengthen STR Enforcement.** Authorities should use inspection powers under the Community Tourism Development Act (2022), coordinating with property valuators to identify unregistered STR units and bring them into compliance with mandatory registration requirements.

**7. Establish a Landlord-Tenant Tribunal.** A dedicated tribunal to resolve rental disputes would reduce the institutional risks that push landlords toward STR conversion, making long-term renting a more secure and attractive investment for property owners.

**8. Legal Requests for Platform Data.** The government should issue formal legal requests to STR platforms for listing and booking data, cross-referenced against the weekly guest reports required under Section 19 of the Tourism Levy Act, to ensure full remittance of nightly levies and to create a longitudinal constituency-level listing dataset. Such data would validate the IDDetail-based STR measure used in this thesis, support ongoing monitoring of platform penetration, and ultimately enable researchers to decompose the reduced-form causal effect estimated here into its STR platform component and its broader employment intensification component.

## Limitations and Future Research

Three limitations constrain the current analysis. First, the endogenous variable — STR unit count from the IDDetail building enumeration — captures a point-in-time snapshot from 2022 and cannot trace the year-on-year expansion of platform penetration across the 2014–2022 treatment period. A longitudinal STR series, which could be assembled from platform data under recommendation 8 above, would allow a cleaner difference-in-differences design exploiting the timing of platform entry.

Second, the pre-period placebo regression (2001–2010) — the most direct test of the exclusion restriction — cannot be executed until the 2001 Saint Lucia Population and Housing Census microdata are obtained from the CSO. The activation code is included in Section 5 pending receipt of that file.

Third, the identification strategy cannot isolate the specific contribution of STR platform activity from the broader economic intensification of tourism corridors. Constituency-level labour force data from the National Insurance Corporation, linked to the census microdata, would allow this decomposition in future work.

Notwithstanding these limitations, this study provides the most rigorous causal analysis of STR platform effects on housing markets in a Caribbean Small Island Developing State to date, and its findings have direct implications for housing policy in Saint Lucia and analogous tourism-dependent island economies across the region.

\newpage

# References {.unnumbered}

Autor, D., Palmer, C., & Pathak, P. (2014). Housing market spillovers: Evidence from the end of rent control in Cambridge, Massachusetts. *Journal of Political Economy*, 122(3), 661–717.

Barron, K., Kung, E., & Proserpio, D. (2021). The effect of home-sharing on house prices and rents: Evidence from Airbnb. *Marketing Science*, 40(1), 23–47.

Bartik, T. J. (1991). *Who Benefits from State and Local Economic Development Policies?* Kalamazoo, MI: W.E. Upjohn Institute for Employment Research.

Baum-Snow, N., & Ferreira, F. (2015). Causal inference in urban and regional economics. In G. Duranton, J. V. Henderson, & W. C. Strange (Eds.), *Handbook of Regional and Urban Economics* (Vol. 5, pp. 3–68). Elsevier.

Borusyak, K., Hull, P., & Jaravel, X. (2022). Quasi-experimental shift-share research designs. *Review of Economic Studies*, 89(1), 181–213.

Briguglio, L., Cordina, G., Farrugia, N., & Vella, S. (2009). Economic vulnerability and resilience: Concepts and measurements. *Oxford Development Studies*, 37(3), 229–247.

Caribbean Development Bank. (2019). *Caribbean Housing Finance: Expanding Access and Affordability*. Bridgetown: CDB.

Desmond, M. (2016). *Evicted: Poverty and Profit in the American City*. New York: Crown Publishers.

ECLAC. (2018). *Caribbean Outlook 2018: Averting the Perfect Storm*. Port of Spain: ECLAC Subregional Headquarters for the Caribbean.

Garcia-López, M.-À., Jofre-Monseny, J., Martínez-Mazza, R., & Segú, M. (2020). Do short-term rental platforms affect housing markets? Evidence from Airbnb in Barcelona. *Journal of Urban Economics*, 119, 103278.

Glaeser, E. L., & Gyourko, J. (2003). The impact of building restrictions on housing affordability. *Federal Reserve Bank of New York Economic Policy Review*, 9(2), 21–39.

Goldsmith-Pinkham, P., Sorkin, I., & Swift, H. (2020). Bartik instruments: What, when, why, and how. *American Economic Review*, 110(8), 2586–2624.

Gotham, K. F. (2005). Tourism gentrification: The case of New Orleans' Vieux Carré (French Quarter). *Urban Studies*, 42(7), 1099–1121.

Gurran, N., & Phibbs, P. (2017). When tourists move in: How should urban planners respond to Airbnb? *Journal of the American Planning Association*, 83(1), 80–92.

Gyourko, J., & Molloy, R. (2015). Regulation and housing supply. In G. Duranton, J. V. Henderson, & W. C. Strange (Eds.), *Handbook of Regional and Urban Economics* (Vol. 5, pp. 1289–1337). Elsevier.

Horn, K., & Merante, M. (2017). Is home sharing driving up rents? Evidence from Airbnb in Boston. *Journal of Housing Economics*, 38, 14–24.

Koh, E., & King, B. (2017). Accommodating the sharing revolution: A qualitative evaluation of the impact of Airbnb on Singapore's budget hotel sector. *International Journal of Contemporary Hospitality Management*, 29(1), 400–415.

Lee, D. (2016). How Airbnb short-term rentals exacerbate Los Angeles's affordable housing crisis: Analysis and policy recommendations. *Harvard Law and Policy Review*, 10, 229–253.

Pelling, M., & Uitto, J. I. (2001). Small island developing states: Natural disaster vulnerability and global change. *Environmental Hazards*, 3(2), 49–62.

Rosenthal, S. S. (2014). Are private markets and filtering a viable source of low-income housing? Estimates from a "repeat income" model. *American Economic Review*, 104(2), 687–706.

Saint Lucia Central Statistical Office. (2022). *2022 Population and Housing Census: Preliminary Report*. Castries: CSO.

Scitovsky, T. (1954). Two concepts of external economies. *Journal of Political Economy*, 62(2), 143–151.

Sheppard, S., & Udell, A. (2016). *Do Airbnb Properties Affect House Prices?* Williams College Department of Economics Working Papers.

Wachsmuth, D., & Weisler, A. (2018). Airbnb and the rent gap: Gentrification through the sharing economy. *Environment and Planning A: Economy and Space*, 50(6), 1147–1170.

Zervas, G., Proserpio, D., & Byers, J. W. (2017). The rise of the sharing economy: Estimating the impact of Airbnb on the hotel industry. *Journal of Marketing Research*, 54(5), 687–705.
